questions.stem: What is the main purpose of a topic sentence in a paragraph?
options: To give an example that supports the paragraph
To state the central idea that the paragraph develops
To provide a transition to the next paragraph
To list the sources used in the paragraph
questions.explanation: A topic sentence announces the controlling idea of a paragraph, and every other sentence then supports or develops that idea. Giving an example or a transition is the job of supporting or linking sentences, not of the topic sentence itself.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: A paragraph has unity when
options: every sentence relates to a single controlling idea
it begins and ends with the same sentence
it uses many transition words
it contains at least five sentences
questions.explanation: Unity means that a paragraph keeps to one main idea and that every sentence supports that idea. A sentence that wanders to an unrelated point breaks unity. The number of sentences or transitions does not by itself create unity.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Read the paragraph and choose the sentence that breaks its unity: "Regular exercise improves health. It strengthens the heart and lungs. It also helps control weight. My cousin recently bought a new car. Furthermore, exercise lifts the mood."
options: Furthermore, exercise lifts the mood.
My cousin recently bought a new car.
It strengthens the heart and lungs.
Regular exercise improves health.
questions.explanation: The controlling idea is that regular exercise improves health, and every sentence should support it. The sentence about a cousin buying a car has nothing to do with exercise or health, so it destroys the paragraph's unity and should be removed.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Coherence in a paragraph refers to
options: the use of a single long sentence
the logical and smooth flow of ideas from one sentence to the next
the length of the paragraph
the number of difficult words used
questions.explanation: Coherence is the quality of holding together, so that ideas are arranged in a clear order and connect smoothly. Writers achieve it through logical sequence, transitions, and consistent reference. It is about how ideas link, not about vocabulary or length.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best topic sentence for a paragraph about the benefits of reading?
options: Some books are very long and heavy.
Reading offers several important benefits to the mind.
The library opens at eight in the morning.
I read a book yesterday.
questions.explanation: A good topic sentence states a controlling idea broad enough to be developed but focused enough to guide the paragraph. "Reading offers several important benefits" promises a clear line of support. The other options are narrow facts or off the point and cannot organize a paragraph on the benefits of reading.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which transition word best shows that an example is about to follow?
options: For instance
However
Nevertheless
On the contrary
questions.explanation: Transitions signal the relationship between ideas. "For instance" introduces an example that illustrates a point. "However," "nevertheless," and "on the contrary" all signal contrast, not illustration, so they would mislead the reader here.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which transition word best signals a contrast between two ideas?
options: However
Similarly
Moreover
Therefore
questions.explanation: "However" signals that the next idea contrasts with what came before. "Therefore" shows a result, "moreover" adds a similar point, and "similarly" shows likeness, so none of those expresses contrast.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which transition word best shows that a conclusion or result follows from the previous statements?
options: Therefore
In addition
Meanwhile
For example
questions.explanation: "Therefore" introduces a result or conclusion drawn from what was said before. "For example" introduces an illustration, "in addition" adds a point, and "meanwhile" marks time, so none of those shows a conclusion.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: In a well organized paragraph, the supporting sentences should
options: explain, illustrate, or give evidence for the topic sentence
repeat the topic sentence word for word
introduce a completely new topic
contradict the topic sentence
questions.explanation: Supporting sentences develop the controlling idea by explaining it, giving examples, or offering evidence. They must serve the topic sentence rather than introduce a new topic, simply repeat it, or argue against it.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: What is the function of a concluding sentence in a paragraph?
options: To introduce a new main idea
To ask an unrelated question
To list references
To restate or round off the main idea of the paragraph
questions.explanation: A concluding sentence signals the end of a paragraph by restating the main idea in fresh words or drawing the supporting points together. It should not raise a new main idea, which belongs in a separate paragraph.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence would best follow this topic sentence to maintain unity? "Keeping a tidy study desk has clear advantages."
options: The weather has been very hot lately.
Football is a popular sport in Ghana.
A tidy desk helps you find your materials quickly.
My favourite colour is blue.
questions.explanation: To preserve unity, the next sentence must support the controlling idea about the advantages of a tidy desk. "A tidy desk helps you find your materials quickly" gives one such advantage and stays on topic. The other choices introduce unrelated subjects.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which arrangement of ideas best gives a paragraph coherence when describing a process?
options: Chronological order, from the first step to the last
Order from the longest sentence to the shortest
Alphabetical order of the first words
Random order of steps
questions.explanation: Coherence depends on a logical order that the reader can follow. For a process, chronological order from the first step to the last lets the reader move through the stages naturally. Random, length based, or alphabetical ordering would scatter the steps and confuse the reader.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: The repeated use of pronouns and synonyms to refer to the same idea across sentences mainly helps a paragraph achieve
options: coherence
length
a topic sentence
a title
questions.explanation: Consistent reference through pronouns and synonyms ties sentences to a shared idea and helps the reader track it from one sentence to the next. This linking is a tool of coherence. It does not, by itself, supply a topic sentence or a title.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: A paragraph should ideally develop
options: only questions
no main idea at all
as many unrelated ideas as possible
one main idea
questions.explanation: The basic rule of paragraphing is one main idea for each paragraph. Packing in several unrelated ideas breaks unity and confuses the reader, while having no main idea leaves the paragraph without a purpose.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Where is the topic sentence most commonly placed in an academic paragraph?
options: Only in the title
At the beginning of the paragraph
In the exact middle of the paragraph
It must never appear in the paragraph
questions.explanation: In academic writing the topic sentence usually opens the paragraph so the reader knows at once what idea will be developed. It can appear elsewhere for effect, but the beginning is the most common and clearest position.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which sentence is too broad to serve as the topic sentence of a single short paragraph?
options: The entire history of human civilization can be summarized here.
Morning assembly builds a sense of community.
Our school canteen should offer healthier snacks.
Planting trees on campus improves the air.
questions.explanation: A topic sentence must be focused enough to be covered in one paragraph. Promising to summarize the entire history of human civilization is far too broad for a single short paragraph. The other three options each name a manageable idea that one paragraph could develop.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Read the paragraph and choose the sentence that breaks coherence by appearing out of logical order: "First, gather all the ingredients. Next, mix the flour and sugar. Finally, serve the cake. Then bake the mixture for thirty minutes."
options: Then bake the mixture for thirty minutes.
First, gather all the ingredients.
Finally, serve the cake.
Next, mix the flour and sugar.
questions.explanation: Coherence in a process paragraph depends on the steps following their true sequence. The baking step must come before serving, yet the sentence "Then bake the mixture" appears after "Finally, serve the cake." Its position breaks the logical order, so it is the sentence that disturbs coherence.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which transition word best adds a further supporting point to a paragraph?
options: Therefore
Furthermore
However
Although
questions.explanation: "Furthermore" signals that another point is being added in support of the same idea. "However" and "although" introduce contrast, and "therefore" introduces a result, so only "furthermore" performs the adding function.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which of these best describes adequate paragraph development?
options: The main idea is supported with enough relevant details
The paragraph is left with only a topic sentence
The paragraph repeats the same sentence several times
The paragraph contains only a heading
questions.explanation: Development means giving the controlling idea enough relevant support through explanation, examples, or evidence so the reader is convinced. A lone topic sentence, mere repetition, or a bare heading leaves the idea undeveloped.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: In the sentence order of a paragraph, transitional expressions such as "first," "next," and "finally" mainly serve to
options: decorate the paragraph
replace the topic sentence
guide the reader through the sequence of ideas
increase the word count
questions.explanation: Sequencing transitions such as "first," "next," and "finally" mark the order of steps or points and help the reader move smoothly from one to the next, which strengthens coherence. They are not mere decoration and do not replace the topic sentence.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Choose the sentence that would best conclude a paragraph whose topic sentence is "Public libraries serve communities in many ways."
options: In short, public libraries remain a valuable resource for the whole community.
Computers are expensive to maintain.
Yesterday I lost my library card.
The first novel was written long ago.
questions.explanation: A concluding sentence should round off the paragraph by restating its main idea in fresh words. "In short, public libraries remain a valuable resource for the whole community" echoes the controlling idea and signals closure. The other options drift to unrelated points and would not conclude the paragraph.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which statement about paragraph length is most accurate?
options: A paragraph must always have exactly three sentences.
A paragraph should be long enough to develop its main idea fully.
A paragraph should contain only one word.
A paragraph should never be shorter than one full page.
questions.explanation: There is no fixed number of sentences for a paragraph. What matters is that it contains enough to develop its single main idea fully and no unrelated material. Rigid rules about an exact count or a minimum page length are not sound guidance.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Read the paragraph: "Bees are vital to farming. They pollinate many crops that feed us. Their work increases harvests. ___" Which sentence best completes it while keeping unity?
options: The film starts at seven tonight.
I once visited a large city by train.
My uncle prefers tea to coffee.
Without bees, many of these crops would fail.
questions.explanation: To keep unity, the closing sentence must continue the idea that bees are vital to farming. "Without bees, many of these crops would fail" follows directly from the point about pollination and harvests. The other options introduce subjects unrelated to bees or farming and would break unity.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide

questions.stem: Which revision improves coherence between these two sentences? "The road was flooded. We arrived late."
options: The road was flooded. We arrived late.
The road was flooded, so we arrived late.
The road was flooded we arrived late.
The road was flooded. Late we arrived.
questions.explanation: Coherence improves when the logical link between ideas is made explicit. Joining the sentences with "so" shows that the flooding caused the late arrival, which the bare pair only implies. The run together version and the awkward reordering do not clarify the connection and harm readability.
questions.source: in the style of a standard English usage guide
